# Verify `sesam init` on an already-initialized repo (a fresh clone) switches
# to setup mode: it wires up this clone's local git integration and reveals the
# secrets the caller can access, instead of trying to create a new repo.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

# Admin creates the repo, adds a secret, onboards bob, commits.
exec sesam init --user admin
cp secret.env app.env
exec sesam add app.env --group dev
# Bob is a non-admin dev: he can read app.env (dev) but not the admin-only
# README init creates. Setup's reveal must skip the README and still reveal
# app.env (RevealAll skips inaccessible secrets rather than aborting).
exec sesam tell --user bob --recipient $BOB_PUBKEY --group dev
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam with a secret and bob'

# Clone into a fresh worktree. The clone has no sesam git config: git filters
# live in .git/config, which clone never copies. So nothing is revealed yet.
exec git clone . clone_bob
cd clone_bob
! exec git config --local filter.sesam-filter.process
! exists app.env

# `sesam init` detects the existing repo and runs setup: wires git and (since
# bob is a recipient) reveals his secrets.
exec sesam --identity $BOB_KEY init
stdout 'Adjusting git config'
stdout 'Revealing secrets'
exec git config --local filter.sesam-filter.process
stdout 'sesam smudge'
exists app.env
grep 'super-secret' app.env

cd $WORK

# A non-recipient (carol) gets git wired but no reveal - and setup still
# succeeds, because the best-effort reveal must never fail the wiring.
exec git clone . clone_carol
cd clone_carol
exec sesam --identity $CAROL_KEY init
stdout 'wired up'
exec git config --local filter.sesam-filter.process
stdout 'sesam smudge'
! exists app.env

-- secret.env --
TOKEN=super-secret
